CENTENARY OF AUBREY DE VERE.

On the 10th day of Januwary, a century ago, Aubrey Thomas De
Vere was born at his ancestral home, Curragh Chase, Co. Limerick, a
fitting birth place for a poet. He was the third son of Sir Aubrey
De Vere, a pooet of no mean ment, a friend and disciple of Words-
worth. As a boy, the younger Aubrey, although of studious habits and
nurtured in a literary atmosphere, gave httle indication of future
greatness ; indeed, his tutor once called him ‘‘an jdiot,”” but soon had
reason to withdraw the harsh epithet. As a youth he became ac-
quainted with and greatly admired Gerald Griffin, who confided to him
that his ambition was to become the Irish Burns. In his eighteenth
vear Aubrey began to write poetry, contributing to the ‘‘National
Magazine,”” the successor of the ‘‘ Dublin Literary Gazette,”” in which
some of his father’s finest sonnets had appeared. He entered T.C.D.
in 1832, with a view to taking orders, and found a congenial friend in
Sir W. R. Hamilton, the poet astronomer. He graduated in 1837, and
in the following year, at Oxford, he met Newman, who became his
life-long friend. Toreign travel, visits to Wordsworth, and jntellectual

association with, Tennyson, Browming, Carlyle, Manmning, indeed, all

the great literary lights of the Victorian era, widened his horizon.
The famine years found him at home doing yeoman service on relief
committees, and the scenes he witnessed seared his soul and impelled
him to pen his fine poem, ‘“ A Year of Sorrow,” and his first prose
work, * English Misrule and Imsh Misdeeds,” in 1848, a sad year,

brightened only by a lengthy visit from Tennyson. Tn 1851 De Vere

was received into the Catholic Church by (Cardinal) Manning, ab

Avignon, on his way to Rome. When Newman founded the Catholie

University he asked De Vore to deliver a series of lectures on litera-

ture, Dhese he prepared, but an attack of scavlatina through which

ho was assiduously atfended by Newman,, prevented the personal de-
livery of the earlier ones. He was appointed professor of political and
social science there, hut his dubies were nominal, and he resigned in

1858. De Vere never married, and his life passed pleasantly at Cur-
ragh Chase, varied only by his annual visit to London and re-uniop
with his youth’s compeers. As one of the landowning class, it neeq
scarcely be said he viewed recent legislative developments with dis-
way.  Excelling in deeds of kindness to allTaround, his long and pleas-
ant career came to a close a week after he had passed his 88th year,
in 1902. He was a voluminous writer, too much so, perhaps, for his
future fame. His poems were too intellectual ever to become popular
—though he enjoyed much vogue in America, and of late he had to
bear the expense of production himsell. He once jestingly said, *“If

popular man with his associates. Sara Coleridge wrote of him as
““ most amiable, uniting a feminine gentleness and compassionateness
withi the most perfect manliness.”” Coventry Patmore said of him,
‘“ he looked like sumshine,” and a writer in ‘‘ The Spectator’ declared
‘““ he was a man whom to know was a liberal education.”” Of his works
Prof. MacNeile Dixon says: ‘‘In his old Irish lays, heroic in theme,
spiritual in significance, and in his poems which enshrine the tradi-
tions of the medieval church, Mr. De Vere is most at home in spirib
and perhaps is at his best. Here he strikes a note which falls upon
the ear with a mingled solemnity and joyousmness and seems to breathe
the very air of that old world of unconscious saintliness and glad
romance.”’ His life has heen written by J. P. Gunning, Limerick,
1902, and by Wiulfrid Wiard, London, 1904.

THE DUNGANNON WEEKLY MAGAZINLE.

In I.B.L., Vol. III., p. 144, an account is given of ‘‘ The Strabane .

Magazine,”’ which appeared monthly throughout the year 1800. It is
a curious fact and one little known, that in the same County of Tyrone,
at the same time, there existed another periodical, entitled ‘‘ The Dun-
gannon Weekly Magazine,”’ a state of things not equalled by any other
country in Jreland during the 18th century. This latter must be the
rarer, for whilst twelve numbers of the former are known to exist
so far as can be discovered, only two of the latter are known. These
belong to Mr. Delmege Trimble, editor and proprietor of *‘ The Armagh
Guardian,” who has kindly sent them for inspection. They are num-
bered respectively Vol. I., No. xiv., May 1, 1800, and No. xvi., May
15, 1800, whioh would show that the first number appeared on lst Feb-
ruary, 1800. Cotton gives the earliest date for Dungannon printing as
1817, but Mr. Dix shows that the art was practised in the town In
1797. So that in about three years from the earliest known date, the
enterprising printer was encouraged to issue a weekly magazine ; &
risky undertaking centainly, but one which speaks well for the progress
and taste of the *‘ Town of the Volunteers.” The magazine is exactly
the size of 1.B.I.., 16 pp., in double columns and wrapper, but folds in
fours, and the imprint is ** Dungannon: Printed by W. Canming.”
The earlier nmumber gives an account of the capture of Seringapatam,
with anecdotes of Tippo Saib, Bonaparte, and the Archduke Charles,

and an article on tho * Causo of the Delay in Opening the Campaign,’’

by the Austrians against Germany, whilst “A T'riend of Truth’’ enters
into o long defence of I'reemasonry in a letter to the Tditor.

There is news from ,Paris, Holland, Germany, and London, but tho
“ Domestic Intelligence,’”’ i.e., from Dublin, is limited to about half w
dozen lines, announcing that Alderman Charles Thorpo had been elect-
ed chief m'lgl%rmte on 25th April, and that the Assize of the Twelvo-
penny Household Loaf is 21h. 150z. On back of wrapper the Proprietor

don Gazette.”” The ‘‘Domestic Intelligence’ is a little fuller, and

Rt. Hon. Thos. Conolly has vacated his seat for Co. Derny, and recom-
mends as his successor Lt.-Col. C. W. Stewact, brother of ILord

appeal to his subscribers was responded to as well as it deserved. We
fear not, and that it must have come to am early end is evidenced by

OBITUARY.

Patrick WEestoN Jovce, LL.D., educationalist, scholar, historian
and musician, passed away at his residence, Leinster Road, Rath-
mines, on 7th Januany, to the great regret of every lover of Irish hit-
erature and music. Born at Ballyorgan, Co. Limerick, in 1827, he waa
educated at private schools, and afterwards proceeded to T.C.D., wnere
he graduated B.A., 1861, M.A., 1864, and LL.D., 1870. 1In 1845 he
had entered the service of the Natiomal Education Commissioners of
Ireland, and by sheer merit and ability rose to the rank of professor
in, and finally principal of the Marlborough Street Training Schools,
Dublin. He retired in 1893, and thenceforth devoted himself to the
more serious study of Irish literature, archeology and musie, which he
had hitherto pursued as holiday tasks. He was elected M.R.I.A. 1n
1863, and became an active member of R.S.A.I., of which he sas
President, 1906-8. A prolific and versatile writer, and as popular as
prolific, one of his works adapted for educational purposes—‘‘ A Child’s
History of Ireland ”’—sold 86,000 copies, and another—*‘‘ Outlines of
History of Ireland ”—over 70,000, records surely for any Irish author.
He was one of the Commissioners for the publication of the Ancient

TO WILLTAM WATSON.
(On the birth of his daughter.)
Oh, noble English poet!—you who sang
In trumpet tones of Ireland, and the day
When the fierce hatred of eight hundred years
Scorched by the light of love must pass away.

Whose glorious tribute on the grave of Burns
Proved your rich gemus and your heart of ruth;
Whose songs against oppression taught us first—
Like Emerson—to covet only truth.

There by the cradle where your daughter lies,
Joy in your joy firom my glad heant has sped
(Who loved the mellow music of your rhymo

And faintly followed where the master led).

S0 short a space upon our faulty earth,

Yet rich already in a noble name,

Crowned with a double glory at her hinth——
Her mother’s beamty and her father’s fame.

RANDALL McDONNELL.

Rosanna, to Miss Ponsonby, one of the famous ‘ Ladies of Llangollen.’

¢ Rosana, 2lst April, 1798, after detailing items of domestic news,

have led me hitherto to give the years (1802?) 1810 as the earliest dates

est hint as to the mature or scope of the work at press, so we are lefi
to conjecture. Mrs. Sarah Tighe was then a widow rvesiding at Ros-
anna, which she refused to quit notwithstanding the disturbed stato

insurgents. Her son Henry was the hushand of the authoress of
“ Pyscho,” and we know that ¢ during this time Mrs. Tighe lived at
Rosanna with her mother-indlaw.”” (Illustrious Ivishwomen, vol. 1L}
Could the book have heen the much debated first: edition of Psyche,”’
usually attnibuted to 1795, being privately printed under the author’s
